summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing a significant energy affordability crisis, with average residential electric bills rising 17.5% in a single year (2024–2025)—the most severe increase in at least two decades—driven by cascading rate approvals for all five major investor-owned utilities. In response, the Indiana General Assembly passed House Enrolled Act 1002 (signed February 2026), which introduces performance-based ratemaking and new low-income customer protections, while the IURC launched a landmark investigative inquiry into the 'big five' utilities in March 2026. Compounding affordability pressures are federal emergency orders keeping aging coal plants online at substantial cost to consumers, and rapidly growing energy demand from AI data centers that advocates warn risks shifting costs onto residential ratepayers.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Low-income ratepayers and households facing disconnection
messaging_recommendations.message: Indiana's Energy Assistance Program (EAP/LIHEAP) can help you pay your heat and electric bills, and the new state law (HEA 1002) prohibits summer shutoffs for eligible low-income customers and requires utilities to offer assistance programs—know your rights and apply every fall when the portal reopens.

Indiana residential electric bills have climbed 28.9% since 2014, far outpacing inflation and peer states; the tools in HEA 1002 are a vital first step, but removing automatic cost-tracker mechanisms and strengthening IURC's authority to reject unjustified rate hikes are essential to making affordability reform durable.

Every major Indiana electric utility raised rates significantly in 2024–2025, and data center growth now threatens to shift even more costs onto households; Hoosiers deserve transparent billing, genuine accountability from monopoly utilities, and assurance that large industrial customers pay their fair share of grid costs.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Community action agencies and social service providers
messaging_recommendations.message: With the 2025–2026 EAP application cycle closed and bills at record highs, connect clients now with utility low-income assistance programs mandated under HEA 1002, and document the human cost of energy burden to strengthen IURC's investigative inquiry record ahead of the Fall 2026 EAP reopening.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana's first base rate case since 2019, requesting an approximately 16.2% increase in retail revenues. The IURC approved an increase of approximately $295.7 million in annual revenues (reduced from Duke's request of ~$491.5 million), implemented in phases through March 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately $295.7 million annual revenue increase (~20% residential bill increase); Duke initially sought ~$491.5 million.

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed for a $192.9 million base rate increase in 2025. A partial settlement agreement among AES Indiana, industrial customers, Walmart, and the City of Indianapolis proposes a $90.7 million increase; the OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition oppose the settlement. The IURC deadline for a final order is June 24, 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Settlement proposes $90.7 million annual revenue increase; OUCC recommends a $21.2 million rate reduction instead.

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO's 2024–2025 electric base rate case, in which most parties including the OUCC reached a settlement agreement. The IURC approved a ~$257 million annual revenue increase (reduced from NIPSCO's initial $368.7 million request), phased in beginning July 2025 through Q1 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately $257 million annual revenue increase; average residential customer sees ~$23/month (16.75%) increase, phased in through Q1 2026.

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO natural gas base rate case filed in 2023–2024. The IURC approved a settlement resulting in a $120.9 million increase (reduced from NIPSCO's initial larger request), with rates phased in through March 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: $120.9 million annual increase; average residential customer sees ~$5.40/month increase.

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy Indiana's electric base rate case requesting an $118.8 million (16.02%) increase. The IURC approved an $80 million increase via a settlement with industrial customers; the increase is being phased in over multiple steps, with a second phase taking effect in early 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: $80 million annual revenue increase approved; average residential customer saw ~$11.18/month increase in step one; second step took effect early 2026.

articles.headline: Utility accountability starts with better public policy
articles.summary: An opinion piece examining the March 2026 IURC investigative inquiry into Indiana's five largest utilities, arguing that regulators need stronger authority to reject unjustified rate hikes and require more from utilities on behalf of residential customers. The piece notes that utility companies blamed 'market shifts' and customer usage for high bills while regulators conducted unprecedented public questioning. It calls for structural policy reforms to accompany the new HEA 1002 law.

articles.headline: Statehouse push for affordable power collides with data center growth
articles.summary: Indiana's new law (HEA 1002) is designed to hold utilities accountable for affordability, but simultaneous state encouragement of energy-hungry AI data centers may undermine those efforts. The article examines the tension between economic development goals and rising residential utility costs, and the IURC's new activist posture under Chair Andy Zay. Advocates warn that data center growth risks shifting substantial grid costs onto residential ratepayers.

articles.headline: 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning
articles.summary: Indiana regulators convened the state's five largest investor-owned utilities for a daylong affordability hearing, pressing them on rate increases, billing transparency, and customer complaints. NIPSCO faced especially pointed questions after the IURC noted it had already received more consumer complaints in 2026 than in all of 2025. Utility executives generally argued that customer usage patterns and infrastructure investment needs drove rising bills.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.summary: IURC Chair Andy Zay announced an 'investigative inquiry' into the affordability practices of Indiana's five investor-owned electric utilities, calling it the beginning of a process to find short- and long-term solutions to rising bills. The inquiry's second phase included 10 community listening sessions across the state in March and April 2026. Zay said the balance between utility needs and customer protection 'feels dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers.'

articles.headline: Gov. Braun says new law will protect Indiana utility customers from rising bills
articles.summary: Gov. Mike Braun ceremonially signed House Enrolled Act 1002 at a Statehouse event, reaffirming his commitment to holding utility companies accountable through new performance-based ratemaking requirements. The law mandates low-income assistance programs, prohibits summer shutoffs for eligible low-income customers, and introduces three-year multi-year rate plans. Braun said it would be 'up to his administration to implement it.'

articles.headline: Utilities warn of soaring costs related to Trump order for Indiana coal plants to stay open
articles.summary: Federal emergency orders from the U.S. Department of Energy are requiring NIPSCO and CenterPoint Energy to keep aging coal plants online months after their planned retirement, and both utilities warned the IURC that compliance costs are rapidly mounting into the hundreds of millions. Keeping NIPSCO's Schahfer plant operational costs consumers an estimated $174,000 per day, according to a Sierra Club analysis. Environmental and consumer advocates have filed legal challenges to the orders.

articles.headline: Hoosiers' electric bills spike 17.5% in one year
articles.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Indiana Public Media
articles.summary: A Citizens Action Coalition analysis found Indiana's average residential electric bill rose 17.5% (about $28/month) in a single year, the most severe increase in at least two decades. NIPSCO customers saw the highest jump at 26.7%, followed by CenterPoint at 25%, Duke at 20%, and AES at 12%. CAC called the trend 'nothing more than lip service' from state leaders who claimed to be prioritizing affordability.

articles.headline: Your electric bill is going to change. New law aims to stabilize utilities
articles.summary: Indiana's House Bill 1002 (now law) introduces several key changes to electric utility regulation, including requiring utilities to submit three-year rate plans, tying rates to performance metrics, mandating regular affordability data reporting, and requiring all residential customers to be enrolled in levelized billing (with an opt-out option). The law is intended to address pocketbook issues as energy demand and bills continue to rise. Some lawmakers said the measures do not go far enough to help everyday Hoosiers.

articles.headline: 'A real short-term crisis here': Regulators press Indiana utilities on rising energy costs
articles.summary: IURC commissioners used the March 24, 2026 affordability hearing to press utilities on their high rates, billing transparency, and customer service amid mounting public frustration. NIPSCO announced during the hearing that it would extend its winter disconnection moratorium through May 15, 2026, and waive reconnect fees for eligible customers. The second phase of the IURC inquiry, consisting of 10 community listening sessions, was announced to run from late March through late April 2026.

articles.headline: High Indiana NIPSCO utility bills a yearslong affordability crisis, advocates say
articles.summary: Citizens Action Coalition Executive Director Kerwin Olson told Fox Chicago that NIPSCO customers are experiencing a 'perfect storm' of infrastructure investment costs and favorable state laws that limit the IURC's ability to hold utilities accountable. Olson said NIPSCO is 'the poster child for cost shifting from industrial customers on to residential customers,' citing declined industrial load and rising residential bills. State lawmakers are reportedly working on legislative fixes as constituent complaints about NIPSCO surge.

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chair
stakeholders.quote: That balance feels dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers and today is the beginning of a process that we are labeling an investigative inquiry. … We're asking our five investor-owned utilities that we regulate on a week-in, week-out basis … to come to this room and have an open dialog about affordability.

stakeholders.name: Anthony Swinger
stakeholders.title: Commissioner
stakeholders.quote: If you look at the total number of CAD complaints this year, it's at the same level that it was for all 12 months (of) 2025 — with more than half the complaints received statewide about NIPSCO. (Paraphrased from direct quote at the March 24, 2026 affordability hearing.)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle, reporting on the March 24, 2026 IURC affordability hearing

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: AES Indiana has not demonstrated sufficient evidence for an increase in this rate case, which it filed less than 14 months after receiving Commission approval in its last rate case. My team is recommending additional reductions that would reduce the utility's current rates and help lessen the burden on ratepayers.
stakeholders.source: OUCC news release / Indiana Capital Chronicle

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: Things keep getting worse for Hoosier consumers and our state leaders continue to allow it to happen. Based on this trend, the rhetoric regarding affordability emanating from the Statehouse appears to be nothing more than lip service. Hoosier consumers can no longer afford monopoly pricing. Something must change, soon.
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Indiana Public Media, reporting on CAC bill analysis

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: I think those customers up there have just sort of experienced the perfect storm. All the money that they spend, they collect from their customers. So they're experiencing the culmination and the pancaking of significant investment with favorable state laws.

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: AI data center issues, to us, represent the single biggest threat to consumer affordability as well as climate change today.
stakeholders.source: Citizens Action Coalition Citizen Power newsletter, May 2026 / WFIU Noon Edition

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: NIPSCO is the poster child for cost shifting from industrial customers on to residential customers. There's a massive outcry right now. We've lost a lot of trust with NIPSCO.
stakeholders.source: Latitude Media, reporting on NIPSCO data center proceedings

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: Affordability is why I support Rep. Alaina Shonkwiler's bill to hold investor-owned utility companies accountable to provide affordable energy before they can increase their profit margin. And it's why I appointed three ratepayer-conscious members, now constituting a majority, to the Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission — to make decisions in the public interest to ensure utilities provide service at just and reasonable rates.
stakeholders.source: Governor Braun's 2026 State of the State Address

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: I want to strongly praise Chairman Zay and the entire Commission for taking a hard stand and demanding real accountability. They asked the tough questions and made it clear that automatic pass-throughs and unchecked hikes are no longer acceptable. Residential electric rates have climbed 28.9 percent since 2014 — far outpacing inflation and our peer states.
stakeholders.source: WFIE / 14News, reporting on IURC affordability hearing

stakeholders.name: Alaina Shonkwiler
stakeholders.title: State Representative (R-Noblesville)
stakeholders.quote: Hoosiers are still feeling the financial burden of heating, cooling and lighting their homes. We are updating our regulatory tools so utilities are held accountable for delivering the outcomes we want: strong reliability, improved resilience and better affordability.
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle, news conference on HB 1002

stakeholders.name: Vince Parisi
stakeholders.title: President and Chief Operating Officer
stakeholders.quote: The investments that we make in our system I think are critical. We talk about customer affordability, you have to talk about in the same breath as reliability, resiliency.

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: AES Indiana's residential bills have largely tracked inflation over time. (Paraphrased from testimony at the March 24, 2026 IURC affordability hearing.)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle, reporting on the March 24, 2026 IURC hearing
